steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a “Support the hypothesis” question. The causal chain in the researchers’ hypothesis is: fewer dusky sharks → more cownose rays → fewer eastern oysters. The missing link the answer must confirm is the middle step: as sharks declined, rays increased.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — “Declines in the regional abundance of dusky sharks’ prey other than cownose rays are associated with regional declines in dusky shark abundance.”: Addresses why shark numbers fell, not the shark–ray–oyster connection. Does nothing to support the hypothesis about oysters. ✗
  • Choice B — “Eastern oyster abundance tends to be greater in areas with both dusky sharks and cownose rays than in areas with only dusky sharks.”: Contradicts the hypothesis. If oysters thrive where rays are present, rays are not suppressing the oyster population as the hypothesis requires. ✗
  • Choice C — “Consumption of eastern oysters by cownose rays in the region substantially increased before the regional decline in dusky shark abundance began.”: Wrong timing. If ray predation on oysters spiked before sharks declined, something other than shark decline drove oyster loss — this weakens, not supports, the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice D — “Cownose rays have increased in regional abundance as dusky sharks have decreased in regional abundance.”: Directly confirms the critical middle step: shark decline → ray increase. More rays would then drive the oyster decline, completing the hypothesis. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
